I really like using the WHOOP app and it has helped me stay consistent with my training, but this is one thing that bothers me.

When I am lifting I am not scrolling on other apps. I am focused on my workout. I would actually prefer to stay in the app and look at my data or adjust my plan between sets. The issue is once I start a workout it locks me into that screen and I cannot move around the app unless I end the session.

That makes it harder to use during training. I want to be able to check my strain or look at my data in real time without stopping the workout. It feels like I have to choose between tracking my session and actually using the app.

I am not asking for distractions, just the ability to move through the app while a workout is running. That would make a big difference for how I train.

review:
I’m writing this on behalf of my 16-year-old son, whose account I manage.
This has been an extremely frustrating and disappointing experience with WHOOP, and it raises serious concerns about how they handle billing and customer communication.
An expiration date was added to the account without authorization, which resulted in a charge we did not approve.
After contacting support, I received written confirmation that the membership would be canceled and that a refund would be issued. I was then told the refund could not be processed unless I withdrew my dispute with the debit card company.
Based on that direction, I withdrew the dispute in good faith so WHOOP could issue the promised refund. As expected, once the dispute was withdrawn, the debit card company ruled in WHOOP’s favor.
After I informed WHOOP that I had done exactly what they asked, I was then told they could not issue the refund because of the debit card company’s ruling.
So to be clear: A refund was promised → I was instructed to remove the dispute → I complied in good faith → and then the refund was denied anyway.
That is not just frustrating, it is completely unacceptable. I relied on their written communication, and their position changed the moment it benefited them.
At this point, I am still requesting that WHOOP honor the refund they explicitly committed to.
Until this is resolved, I would strongly caution others to be very careful when dealing with their billing practices and dispute process.
title: Promised refund then it was denied after following their instructions

review:
Update: whoop, who if you look at the recent reviews, backs ups my experience on the tee is now suing Bevel a “competitor” who does not sell hardware and app actually functions and does not freeze. Whoop also just raised a round of funding so it is obvious they need to make more money to satisfy their shareholder which means the consumer is now on the bottom of their list to support. Stay away!!

Where to start…
I started with the Whoop 3.0 and liked it’s features and easy to use. Bought 18 month subscription and $200 in straps and batteries. 45 days later 4.0 comes out and is free and all gear I bought is obsolete! No notice. No refund. 

1. Whoop also double dips. Charge $30/ month for service then sells our info “anonymously” for more profit!! Why so expensive then!
2. Constant hardware issues. Battery doesn’t fully charge Whoop and could not get a spare battery for 8+ months. Battery updates have come out with no resolve so proves there is a known issue. 
3. Customer service is horrible and wants to just replace unit for every issue but only complimentary first time. Also takes time to respond but then wants to close ticket within 24 hours if you don’t respond asap. 
4. If you are serious about using this to work out, real time data is important. In version 4.0 constant data sync issues. I wake up and takes 1-2 hours for info to update. How can I know where my body is at and plan my day/ workout with that? I’m paying for this info too at a premium. 
5. App AI is confusing to navigate and overwhelming. Once you figure it out another update comes out and you start all over again. 

Bottom line, Whoop started off strong and great info for athletes. As of late it’s about profits and growing it’s wearables line but app and hardware have dropped wayside. I’m not renewing subscription and sad to see such a great source of info fail slowly.

review:
This gadget is absolutely incapable of accurately tracking your sleep. 
If you’re going through menopause, struggling with sleep, and thinking this bracelet might help… don’t bother and save yourself $300!

If you wake up in the middle of the night, stay awake for a while, and then fall back asleep, the device doesn’t detect that you went back to sleep. In the morning, it tells you that your recovery was 34%. But if you manually enter your actual wake-up time, magically your recovery jumps to 93%—completely ignoring the fact that you were awake for hours during the night.

Or picture this: you have a restless night, wake up 50 times, toss and turn in bed, and finally fall into deep sleep around 5:30 AM. Just then, the bracelet vibrates on your wrist and wakes you up in the middle of a dream, leaving you groggy and exhausted. You think it’s the latest time in your smart alarm window (9:15 AM)… but it’s only 8:15! You’re left thinking you must’ve recovered well—though you feel awful. You open the app, and it tells you: “We’re currently unable to detect data.” So… why did you wake me up??

And when you finally manage to sleep well for once, and wake up truly rested… the device tells you your recovery is only 62% because your Heart Rate Variability dropped. Seriously?! Who cares! I finally slept well and feel great!

I used to think the Apple Watch was bad… but compared to this Whoop, it deserves an honorary degree!
title: Disappointed by Whoop: Save Your $300!

review:
I give this product zero stars because it is a low-quality device—cheap, inaccurate, unreliable, and simply not fit for purpose.

From the first day, I reported serious issues with core features such as ECG and blood pressure, which do not work at all. These are not minor features—they are exactly why someone would pay for a WHOOP membership. Despite raising these issues early, nothing was fixed.

Basic functionality is also unacceptable. I walk around 10,000 steps daily, yet the device records nothing while I am walking unless I manually log the activity. That completely defeats the purpose of using a wearable tracker.

The overall experience feels like using a product that is far behind the market standard, both in hardware quality and software performance. It is nowhere near the level of competitors like Apple Watch or Oura.

Customer support makes things worse. Responses is an AI automated and repetitive, with no real effort to resolve the actual problem. Instead of fixing issues, they redirect to generic troubleshooting steps that do not work.

WHOOP even granted me an additional one-month credit, which clearly acknowledges there were unresolved issues. Yet when I requested a refund, they refused, relying strictly on policy rather than addressing the fact that the product never worked properly from the start.

In summary:
	•Core features do not function properly
	•Tracking is highly inaccurate
	•Product quality is poor
	•Support is inhuman and ineffective and unhelpful
	•Refund handling is unfair

This is not a premium product. It is an underperforming device sold with a subscription model that does not deliver on its promises.

I do not recommend it.

review:
The WHOOP 5.0 with the Life subscription has been an impressive experience so far. The app is beautifully designed, intuitive, and the new AI features are outstanding. It analyzes your metrics in context, connects the dots across sleep, strain, recovery, and Labs results, and gives you meaningful, actionable insights that actually help you improve. It feels like having a personal performance coach in your pocket.

That said, there are two areas that need attention. First, the price is high — the value is there, but it’s still a significant investment. Second, the lack of an Apple Watch app is a real miss. When running, there’s no way to monitor recommended strain levels or stay within your limits unless you carry your phone, which isn’t practical for many of us. Adding Apple Watch support would take the experience to another level.

Overall, an excellent platform with huge potential, but I hope future updates address these gaps.

I really loved Whoop…at first. To be honest, I still want to. They do a great job with data visualization and using AI to motivate users to adopt healthier habits. Sounds great right? And it is….until something goes wrong. 

Good luck solving any issues. Because their customer service is THE ABSOLUTE WORST!!!

I’ve been trying diligently for 6 weeks to fix an issue with my Whoop Advanced Labs subscription after my very first round of bloodwork failed to finish updating in the promised 7-10 day timeframe. Actually that seems generous, considering that I actually took the test SIXTEEN WEEKS AGO. I know I’m a procrastinating disaster of a human, but I thought it would work itself out in time and all would be well if I was patient. Huge mistake. 

The subscription promised to give me a health report after that 7-10 days to detail what changes you might implement to improve your next labs. Still haven’t seen that, can’t book my next round of labs until it does. And despite countless emails over more than 6 weeks, nothing has changed. 

Whoop: you have turned your biggest fan into a harsh critic. You should really do better by your premium subscribers.

review:
WHOOP offers top-tier data, but the app is functionally broken if you aren't using default font settings.
I’m over 40 and use Display Zoom/Larger Text on my iPhone. Instead of scaling the UI, WHOOP's views simply run off the edges of the screen. Key metrics and charts become unreadable because they are cut off, making the "awesome data" useless.
I submitted a ticket, and while support was fast, their answer was that this isn't a "bug." If a UI fails to contain its own content when using standard iOS accessibility features, it is absolutely a bug. Until they fix these scaling issues, I can't recommend this for anyone who needs larger text.
title: Great Data, Terrible Accessibility

review:
I rely on WHOOP specifically for sleep and recovery insights, but recently it has been significantly misclassifying awake time as sleep.

On multiple nights where I was clearly awake for extended periods (including nights where I barely slept at all), WHOOP logged full sleep sessions and generated high Sleep Performance scores (80–90%+). When the “hours asleep” metric is wrong, the entire Sleep Performance score becomes unreliable because it’s calculated using:

• Hours vs. Sleep Need
• Sleep Efficiency
• Sleep Consistency
• Sleep Stress

If awake time is incorrectly counted as sleep, both “Hours vs. Needed” and “Sleep Efficiency” are artificially inflated — which then produces a misleadingly positive Sleep Performance score.

Support acknowledged that the score depends on the sleep duration it detects, but the burden is placed on the user to manually edit sleep bounds to correct inaccurate detection. A recovery device at this price point should not require manual correction for basic sleep detection.

If the core sleep detection is inaccurate, the downstream recovery metrics lose credibility.

I hope WHOOP improves its awake vs. asleep classification accuracy, because the hardware and overall concept are strong — but reliable sleep tracking is foundational.
title: Inaccurate Sleep Tracking – Inflated Scores When Awake

review: WHOOP is better than Oura Ring and Apple Watch for weight training because it is designed to be worn during lifting and to actually quantify strength work, not just detect that a workout happened. Oura advises removing its ring during weightlifting due to friction and damage risk, which makes it impractical for consistent strength-training tracking. Apple Watch can record strength workouts, but detailed logging of sets, reps, and weight typically relies on third-party apps, and its battery life (about a day) makes continuous recovery and strain tracking harder. In contrast, WHOOP’s Strength Trainer is built specifically for resistance training, allowing users to log exercises, sets, reps, and weight and translating that data into a muscular load and overall strain score, while its multi-day battery life supports uninterrupted 24/7 wear.
title: The only wearable to use if you train with weights.

review:
WHOOP has been far more than a fitness tracker to me — it has been a partner in my personal rebirth. Over the past year, this tiny band helped me rise into the woman God designed me to be. It showed me the truth about my sleep, my strain, my recovery, and my stress patterns… but more importantly, it revealed a deeper relationship between my faith, my mindset, and my physiology.

My Heart Rate Variability — a real, scientific marker of resilience — didn’t just inch upward… it transformed. It became visible, measurable evidence that my inner peace practice was literally reshaping my nervous system. I wasn’t just feeling different. WHOOP proved that I was different.

Every green recovery felt like God whispering, ‘You’re aligned.’
Every red day reminded me, ‘Rest is sacred.’
And watching my HRV rise became the key to my own inner vault — proof that peace, discipline, and divine alignment can be tracked, trained, and embodied.”

WHOOP helped me regulate my nervous system, rebuild consistency, restore trust with my body, and return to the woman of presence and power I thought I’d lost. It didn’t just optimize my life — it transformed my identity.

If you’re looking for something that doesn’t just measure data but reveals the blueprint of your becoming… WHOOP is it.
title: Daily Rhythm of Peace

review:
So I have been very skeptical of the whoop, so many people hate on it because of the membership and other reasons around accuracy. 

However, I have tested it for myself against my apple watch, my oura ring, my garmin and coros watch lol. 

Honestly apple is the best when it comes to HR tracking. But they do not give you any insights, or use any sort of algorithmic analysis tool which is really where the magic happens. 

Whoop is absolutely accurate enough on Hr data for the use case’s its intended for. Pair that with the algorithms they use and constant analysis against many other markers and you have the best health and fitness tool on the market. Ive seen countless videos on youtube saying how apple watch with athylitic or bevel are just as good if not better. I personally do not agree. Those apps dont have great UX, the results are confusing and dont seem super accurate to me. However they compile data and analyze it just isnt at the level of whoop. 

The whoop is comfortable and thr app is really amazing. I love the interface, the data makes a lot of sense and its easy to understand and make actionable choices with it. Its super focused on gives me exactly what i need and nothing more that i dont. I hear some people asking for nutrition tracking… please dont lol its just another manual task that is calorie focused and not very accurate in my opinion. It kind of grtd away from thr whole athletics and performance tracker and makes it more of a wellness tool. 

Keep up the great work, I love everything about the whoop mg and what it has to offer, there isnt a feature on here I dont use.
title: Really is the best

review:
I wish I could have a more positive review about this Whoop Band. Over the months that I have used this device, it has slowly degraded over time. Constant connectivity issues, and sleep metrics are not recorded. Sometimes I am unable to save my workouts on the app via strength trainer. Which gets pretty annoying after five minutes of trying to save a workout. 

Of the random disconnections Sometimes the band does not get recognized by Bluetooth whatsoever, even if the device is charged. I’ve also heard negative results from the 5.0 that’s the same fate. It is unlikely that I will be using who after another year, seeing is that I spend more time focused on the whoop app and analyzing the metrics rather than not looking at it. 

Also, the sleep alarm does not save at certain times which is very annoying when you’re trying to go to sleep.
title: Connectivity

review:
I generally don’t leave reviews of any kind, but my recent experience with their customer service made me think I should. 
First the good things. Comfortable and non intrusive to wear. But that is where it ends. The much touted recovery feature isn’t all that. I regularly ignore it, as well as the daily strain recommendations, and it seems to have no impact and I am no spring chicken. 

Having used it for around an year, as an average fitness enthusiast who gets in 4-6 intense workouts a week, there aren’t any other metrics you can’t get elsewhere, for a fraction of the price. 

Next, any upgrades will render your existing accessories like bands (which come at an extra cost) useless, without disclaimers before you upgrade, because they change the size of their core product. 

Customer service, when contacted about the issue above, said they were ‘glad’ to tell me that the unused accessory can’t be used on the upgraded version (I am sure they were glad). Additionally, their solutions were either for me to not use the upgrade I paid for already and go back to the older version or reel in another fish for them who can pay them subscription money while using the band I paid for.

TLDR
Functionality:4
Value for money:1
Customer Service: abysmal and tone deaf, 0
title: Not worth it for the average fitness enthusiast

review:
I’ve been using my WHOOP for 42 months now and I used to love it. Have a since they started using AI it’s been nothing but useless inaccurate GARBAGE. Around a year ago my WHOOP showed that my Deep Sleep and REM increased by significantly (30% - 40%) in less than a a couple of months, without me changing anything with my eating, activity, sleep, etc.. 
I’ve had difficulty falling asleep for most of my life, and in the last year or so WHOOP has been regularly showing my sleep Latency to be practically zero nowadays and that’s definitely not been the case. Most of the time it doesn’t even show that I got up to go to the bathroom while I was sleeping and, instead, it shows that I was in Deep Sleep through the entire time that I was awake and walking to/from the bathroom.
I’m extremely disappointed with what WHOOP has become. It’s a complete and worthless piece of garbage! I’m now looking for something else to use instead of it.

review:
Now they don't know how days work - my early morning workouts get assigned to the previous date because I sleep after those workouts. Signed up for a year and absolutely regret it. This whole thing is crazy bad and doesn’t even work well unless you spend hours and hours troubleshooting - if the device lasts as long as my running watch then it’ll cost me over $1k at least. Even if they give you more features and new devices - those features and devices are unwanted and unnecessary so it’s a “value added” but “value” is defined by a product manager somewhere and not by the users. I’m completely dissatisfied with the user experience. So much input required from the user so it just interrupts my day. I’d take error over daily manual input any day. The Fitbit UX in 2015 was miles ahead of this. LLM integration still doesn't work years later.

Trying this out one year later and it's still bad. The LLMs are now integrated but don't work well. There is no more customer service, and instead you just have an LLM hallucinating settings. Not designed for people of color - the sensor struggles so much

review: I first “subscribed” to Whoop in 2019 for 18 months.  I decided to give it another try in Sept 2025 when my AW fell off in the ocean. Recent updates allow partial integration with Apple Health and other health tracking apps. Ive worn the Whoop 4.0, Polar chest strap, Garmin Fenix 8 concurrently with wide variations in HR and most other metrics. At $20-$30 per month, forever, I expect much better ownership and integration of my own data, as well as better accuracy. With the abundant entrants to the health tracking market, I expect much better pricing and better data management from competitors.

After getting advice from their chat bot, I requested by email to speak to a manager (following the bot’s instructions) for help to finally resolve the plethora of issues I’ve had with them.  It’s been silent for weeks. No reply. The AI bot has now actually admitted that the best way to get real help is to make a bad review on a public forum so here I am. review:
I enjoy wearing my Whoop and have gotten my mother one too. But it has challenges syncing and recording activities. When I enter manual activities it always tells me there’s a conflicting activity and I need to delete it and I can’t see where or what (and don’t want to spend the time on editing activities).

The in app guidance like proposed bedtime etc is great. I with it had more of that. I don’t know how to read these data sets like my weekly strain overview lol, what am I to do with this cluttered view with all the colors? I just want to know what the recommendations are as I can’t make sense of how the data is presented. The same goes for several other data sets it collects.

With the new biological age calculator. I got a few things I want to work on but whoop does not do a good job of explaining what they are in that view, eg as I’m looking at the data on HR zones I want to see what those are and what activities I need to do to improve in that same view, and not have to research it separately. 

It’s good that Whoop tracks the data for its recommendations and all the insights the Whoop team delivered to the world, but the app itself needs to be delivered and give real and easily accessible insights to users wearing it. 

When I ask questions to the Whoop AI coach it gives me very general answers not at all tailored to my actual date reflected in whoop.

I know better than to push it right now, but I am hopeful this is a good sign and more green is in my near future. I love how Whoop scans my data and gives me personalized feedback each day. I wish Whoop would use data from a heart rate monitor during workouts. Whoop gives me too much credit for Zones 5 and 6. HRM shows I never got in those zones but Whoop thinks I did. review:
I’ve been really enjoying my Whoop, so much so that I’d love to ditch my Apple Watch altogether. But I’m giving 3 stars because there are still some gaps, especially when it comes to women’s health tracking.

To be clear, the app does offer cycle tracking, which I appreciate, but I still find it quite bare. Personally, one of the most important tools for understanding my body is temperature trend data—specifically basal body temperature. Right now, the app shows a daily temperature reading, but I can’t see past data in a graph or timeline. Without that, I can’t spot patterns, make informed decisions, or understand how my cycle is affecting recovery and performance.

Temperature trends are incredibly informative not just for cycle tracking, but for training adjustments, stress management, fertility awareness, and even postpartum recovery. Whether someone is trying to conceive, manage hormonal fluctuations, or navigate perimenopause, this kind of data matters. And when we can correlate it with metrics like HRV, strain, and recovery, it makes for a much more complete picture of health.

Whoop gets so much right, and I’m hopeful they’ll build this feature in for a more insightful platform. Add this one piece, and it’s a no-brainer 5 stars.
title: Great potential, but still lacking key women’s health features

review:
I just got a new band and a couple shady things that annoy me. The checkout process automatically adds a band and it’s actually confusing, in my opinion, on how to remove it so new users will pay $50 for no reason.
It says all over the place on their marketing that it’s HSA eligible and that was the only reason that I actually want to go for this and then when you put in your HSA card, it doesn’t work.  It’s really confusing why it was rejected and you have to Google around. So apparently you have to submit to HSA which I’m sure a ton of people will forget and I am also sure it’s so whoop saves money no directly processing it themselves.
Seems like a technicality, but I’m sure the amount of people that won’t actually be able to have the time to follow through with this and lose money is substantial.
Also, they don’t actually know if it’s approved until the purchase goes through and the whole submission process happens and if HSA doesn’t cover it. You can’t reverse gears at that point…
Also, I literally had to use AI to understand the difference between MG, 5.0, one, peak and life.
So confusing.
I do think the app is great and it can really help people’s lives but I feel like it’s nickel and diming me everywhere it can.
I have used whoop for 2 or 3 years. But I am skeptical if I will return next year because it feels like too much a dirty subscription based model. Maximizing profit seems slightly more important than helping people. They aren’t always 1 to 1.

Also the steak meter is dumb. That’s a retention feature better suited to video games. I don’t want to see a flame icon on my top left Home Screen in the app, forever. I use whoop everyday I don’t need your cry for keeping users around.  This is why subscription models are awful.

review:
I've been using Whoop on and off for the past five years, and my experience has been largely positive, though my inconsistency in usage has been more about my own habits than anything the company has done. Whoop has continually impressed me with its product and the insights it provides into my health and performance. The data I’ve received has been invaluable, helping me understand my recovery, strain, and sleep patterns better than ever before.

Each iteration of the Whoop band seems to improve upon the last, with enhanced features and more accurate tracking. The insights it provides are not just numbers; they offer actionable information that can help optimize workouts and recovery. However, I do feel that there is room for improvement, particularly in terms of integrations with other fitness services.

For instance, I use a workout tool called Fitbod, which offers in-depth workout planning and insights. It would be fantastic if Whoop could pull data from Fitbod, allowing both platforms to work together seamlessly. The combination of Whoop's recovery insights with Fitbod's detailed workout analytics could create a powerhouse of fitness data, helping users like me get even better results.

Overall, Whoop is an excellent tool for anyone serious about their fitness journey. The insights are top-notch, but more integrations with other fitness technologies would take this product to the next level. If you’re considering Whoop, I highly recommend it, just be prepared to commit to using it consistently for the best results!
title: Great product, Great insights, Missing great integrations

review:
The app gives you decent recovery insights, not much more beyond what a Garmin or Apple Watch would tell you.

The UX is whatever, adding and editing strength workouts is a pain, and the list goes on.

Makes me question the sincerity of the whole operation. Just another money syphon? Seems so with the new pricing tiers through which you have to “unlock” extra measurements that are already there.

Too late for me to return this thing, but I would otherwise.

Here are some issues that I’ve observed:
The activity tracking and app navigation is lacking, plus if you’re not always running the app in the background it can have some serious sync issues.
Honestly, even if you are always running it, it still has sync issues. 
It often chokes on random activities that I add and then takes forever to process the data. Sometimes the same happens with sleep data. 

This never happened on my old Garmin and as it is now, I’m likely going to return.

How data is displayed is also not super intuitive, why do I have this weird live heart rate monitor thing but can’t see a graph with my logged heart rate? Seems suspicious, are they just interpolating a bunch of datapoints between sporadic measurements?

Other issues:
Changing unit system doesn’t work for strength training, can’t search the menu for settings (standard feature by now), no way to give feedback in-app, getting support is just an AI and it’s hard to get somebody to fix issues, some activities/exercises are not in the list and cannot be added manually (archery, and jumping lunges for exercises for example), … there are a few issues that seem like low-hanging fruit considering this is version 5 of this device.
title: Below expectations

title: Too expensive for the quality

review: If you value your health and are hesitant, I will tell you that it’s currently the best wearable you can purchase. It presents you with data which can be easily interpreted. Its journal feature is my favorite which makes tracking activities effortless. These activities/habits range from consuming “x” amount of caffeine on a given day, to having a dog sleep on your bed and how it correlates with your recovery. The battery also lasts a good 4-5 days (Whoops 4.0) and you forget it’s on your wrist. I wear it on my right arm while typically wearing a traditional watch on my left and I forget that it’s there (even when typing on a computer, writing, or sleeping). My Garmin drove me nuts because it was a hunk of plastic when I tried to sleep. It did collect similar data but the way it presents it to the user is what makes Whoop special.  It simply allows you to integrate systems in your life to support your health.

review:
Whoop has great marketing and aesthetics, along with being lightweight. Unfortunately it suffers from poor battery life, and less functionality than products offered from competitors. For example, a lightweight Garmin watch has much better battery life while also having the functionality of a watch. Moreover, the Whoop lacks SpO2 measurements in sleep which makes it a poor option for those who have or had sleep apnea and want to track their oxygen status during sleep. The app is also annoying at times. The subscription model is also more on the annoying side. 
From my own tests, other fitness and health trackers more accurately measure my perceived energy levels and sleep quality. The whoop sleep feels made up to me as it often doesn’t correlate (likely because of SpO2). For these reasons I cannot recommend this product.

review:
This review would have been 5 stars had it been written it in 2021. However, Whoop - their product, their management team, and ultimately their value proposition have changed, and thus so has my perception of it.

I’ve had some great success since joining Whoop in 2020. The device provided some extremely useful insights, particularly around sleep, that I have used to improve my life. 

Whoop as a company was, in my view, consistently on the cutting edge of wearable fitness tech. I believe what once was a laser-focus on product and software development has eroded to one of marketing gimmicks and misinformation. So much of their spending and time now goes into having pro athletes be seen wearing a Whoop as opposed to actually improving product accuracy, performance, and experience.

Further, I cannot in good faith recommend a company who markets a <10% size reduction as a crowning product achievement that took years to develop, when all of its users know it’s simply to make all 4.0 accessories obsolete. 

Whoop has investors to answer to. I get it. It’s a corporate world, but they have been in the process of a multi-year misassessment of their niche in the marketplace. You can’t grow when the bucket is leaking, so I’m voting on what the company has become with my wallet.

Garmin and Apple have not only caught up, but surpassed Whoop in all the ways that made it unique. Their sensors are better, UI/UX equally intuitive and beautiful, and features much broader and more extensive. Oh, and you can get a new one every year as opposed to paying a subscription that no longer covers the cost of new hardware when it releases.
title: 5 Year Customer - Cancelling

review: Got a Whoop 5.0. Ever since my phones battery life is plummeting. Looking in battery and power settings it shows the Whoop app is active all day with 1 minute of screen time and has used 78% of the battery today. Absolutely wild

I’m 2 months into a 12 month contract. The free hardware upgrades sold me not the company and made the decision to commit for a year easy. A similar situation happened when the 4.0 dropped. The policy went from free hardware upgrades to free only if you have 6 months or more left on your contract without notice of change. 

The software is pretty robust and useful. The company cannot be trusted.

review:
I got Whoop for Christmas from my parents. The subscription cost the same as my present budget for both my birthday and Christmas. I willingly gave up my birthday present this year in order to get a Whoop.

I was also excited for the promise of not having to pay for new hardware. However, the Whoop 5.0 came out, and Whoop said “you need to pay for another year of service to get the 5.0 or pay a fee for the new hardware. On top of that, the actual new product is more expensive and we’ll prorate your subscription to get it.”

Okay… I was planning on waiting the month until I had my subscription for the six months originally advertised as the threshold for the upgrade. After such a large backlash, they decided to generously waive the fee if you have 12+ months in your subscription OR if you recently go your Whoop 4.0.

In short, Whoop promised one thing, decided to do something else, and then still screwed me over.

Also, the people that designed this app must hate their users. The messaging in the app always blames the flaws on the user, saying things like "Your Whoop is off body".  No, it's not, it's on my wrist! And there isn't a way in the app to say "No, it's you, wake up you piece of junk."

review: I’m into this a week and it’s been life changing.  I’ve been battling neurological/cardiac symptoms and I’ve been able to accomplish more recovery in 6 days with these metrics than in 6 months of medical treatment.  I’d love to see the ability to add custom activities, though.   As an example, I’m using cold therapy to stimulate the vagus nerve.   I don’t have the ability to track that, electrostim or any of the other processes that seem to generate a lower stress response.  The journal is also insufficient, so I have to use something external with this to track responses to weather (temp), medication, etc.  All-in-all, this is incredible.  Thank you for creating it, I’m getting my life back bc I can finally see what works in real time.

review: I have been a WHOOP member since 2020. It’s my primary heart rate monitor for sleep and activity. I don’t use an Apple Watch or a Garmin HRM. I would say WHOOP HRM works great except for a few things. I think it reads heart rate low sometimes during an activity. It also sometimes doesn’t detect sleep. I like how they’ve integrated AI. I’m staying with WHOOP because I don’t think there is a better option. But I wish they’d make it perfect. It’s not cheap.

review:
Since I have owned my Whoop (5 months) I have had regular issues with it syncing. It seems if you are not constantly on top of updates or willing to reboot it, you go multiple days without your normal data. I’ve contacted Whoop but they don’t give me any more assistance than what their chat bot and FAQ say, and this is something friends have also warned me about.

Additionally, the sync to Strava is also a subpar experience. Unless you track a workout with all your reps and sets live, it will prematurely send your activity to Strava before you have added in your workout to calculate the proper strain and calories, thus making your workouts either slow and distracted, or your Strava data inaccurate. It does not update when you calculate muscular load or offer an option to send to Strava after you track your workout. Additionally, Whoop will automatically track an activity often incorrectly and send this. When you change it, changes not reflected in 3rd party apps it sends to. 

Also, the mapping and GPS capabilities are atrocious. Testing side by side with my Garmin, it is completely inaccurate and only gushes your steps and exertion fairly well. Always short changing and the battery dies fast. Compared to my very simple and easy-to-use Garmin instinct which I’ve had for 6 years and still has a great battery, easy to use and track workouts (including muscular load, reps & weight), Whoop has been more of a frustration. I am mainly using the sleep tracking data and other health monitor info which my Garmin doesn’t have, but instead of adding to my fitness enjoyment, it’s mostly a frustration. 

They also did not honor the referral I gave a friend. His device had Bluetooth issues.
Once he resolved and started using, they refused to give me a free month.
title: Arduous, Buggy, Inaccurate Exercise Tracking & Complex. Garmin Superior.

review: I’ve been wearing my Whoop for over a year and a half now and it’s not only been a perfect bio data collector in its accuracy but it’s also been a huge help in its design for me to take advantage of what is collected to improve my mental and physical health and wellbeing. From improving my sleep to helping me understand my own limitations so that I don’t get sick and overwhelmed by all of life’s challenges. I honestly don’t think that I can imagine ever getting rid of my Whoop. It’s just such an amazing tool for helping me to navigate through life, I don’t want to ever be without it again!

review:
A 1 star review is not a true reflection of my thoughts on the Whoop. Great product, helpful in a ton of ways. The reason I give a 1 star review is because if you’re a potential buyer, you will read the 1 star reviews.

The bracelet set up, especially if you get a band other than the default black, is comically frustrating. For a company that has done so well and has gained so much traction, it is beyond me why they haven’t made the design simpler. The set up itself is next to impossible, and the instructions included are not going to help you. It’s like they hired monkeys to make the bracelet set up, that and the instructions. No joke.

I have it now, and have loved the product itself so far, and would recommend it there, but the set up was so frustrating that that small block of your day, week, month etc. that you spend setting this up, makes it not worth it. It’s unbelievable. It was enough to make me write this review- I’ve never written a review for anything. Cool product, but day 1 you should be prepared to bring a tool belt and be at your sharpest, otherwise you’d best make yourself comfortable because you could end up sitting there all day.

Who am I, but whoever was in charge of that should be bagging groceries because they are not good at what they do. If you get past set up it’s an awesome product with some cool features. Do with this what you will. Cheers
title: Great product, horrible design

review: Taking for ever to open when it opens. Not funny
title: Crashed

review:
I’m starting to get really irritated. I keep getting a network error when trying to convert “Naps” to “sleep”.

review:
First review I’ve ever written for anything but can’t help myself. This is one of the worst designed, most confusing, cognitively taxing, over-stuffed apps I’ve ever used. So much distracting noise hampering usability and drowning out useful insight. My hrv and strain spikes every time I open it. Very disappointing. Redesign desperately needed. 

PS I know how hard it is to design things well, especially in a large, profit focused company. Nevertheless, this app screams “product designed as a reflection of org chart”. Internal incentives of competing teams and product managers is painfully clear. Chief Product Officer needs to step in and focus this back around customer needs; not the goofy ideas of some pm/designer.
title: This app is a nightmare

review: Pain in the butt to figure out how to charge it. The app ONLY works if you have internet connection. The size of the thing is enormous for what it does. Best of all- it didn’t sync ANY of my data after wearing it for a few days. Glitchy. Overpriced. Get a different product for 1/4 of the price that does the same thing but better. Waste of money.

review: Unfortunately, I am cancelling three weeks into the “free” 30-day trial. Oh wait, it's not “free” because I have to pay shipping to return the device.  Ridiculous to have to pay return shipping for a device that often reads my heart rate 15-20 bpm higher than it actually is, significantly overcounts steps and just doesn’t track sleep correctly.  After 5 rounds with customer service where they finally pointed out that the only place they disclose the return shipping charge during thr “free trial” sign-up is buried inside of a link to terms, they finally “as a courtesy” to refund the return shipping charge (I’d already shipped it back).  Appropriate resolution in the end but unreliable product and less-than-transparent marketing practices.

review: The Whoop requires internet to work, which is usually fine. However, lately there have been outages on the app, without prior notice, and I cannot access existing data, log new data, or even contact costumer support — I had to find their number through elsewhere. You want to build a product that needs internet just for it to exist, fine, but make sure it always works. To pay $20/month and this product not work is unacceptable — Whoop isn’t a startup anymore. Might as well buy a Garmin or Apple Watch which can log activities even if the server is down. When it works the product is great, but I am fed up with it not working as often as it has.

review:
As a student in Abnormal Psychology, I found the Whoop app to be equally interesting and relevant in how it relates to topics in my course. Especially concerning stress, anxiety, and sleep disorders. This app tracks physiological data like sleep cycles, heart rate variability, and recovery. Which are all relevant to the biological determinants of stress related conditions and anxiety disorders. Chronic stress for example, poor recovery scores or irregular heart rate patterns can show up due to heightened sympathetic nervous system activity. Heightened nervous system activity is often seen in individuals with PTSD and GAD. 
One of the most useful features of the Whoop app was the sleep coach. This feature aligns closely with disorders covered in class, it supports cognitive-behavioral approaches to insomnia, by encouraging good “sleep hygiene”.  By tracking factors like strain or late-night activity and their impact on sleep quality. This could be helpful for individuals struggling with mood or anxiety disorders, where sleep is often disrupted. 
One thing I have noticed is that this constant monitoring could become counterproductive for some people. In abnormal Psych we have discussed how individuals with OCD or illness anxiety disorder can become hyper focused on their bodily sensations. In this context, an app like this may unintentionally trigger compulsive checking/tracking. While Whoop promotes healthy self-awareness it’s important to recognize the potential for mal use. 
Overall, I’ve found that Whoop bridges the gap between technology and psychology, by offering health insight, and tracking how the body responds to stress and sleep. Whoop provides a great amount of biofeedback that supports wellbeing which could be used to support people struggling with stress, anxiety, and sleep disorders.
title: Whoop app’s potential to support mental health disorders

It had an error close to the end. Refreshed and I had to start over from scratch. This is not helpful. Also have no idea what this is for other than you have my info and what benefits I get for supplying f this info. Not “trying again”.

review:
Signed up for a 1-month trial just to test Wups. Turns out, if you want to cancel the subscription, you’re forced to pay for the remaining 11 months — even if you don’t plan to use them. So trying it for $30 ends up costing $360 just to cancel? What kind of scam is that?

The cancellation page clearly states you can only cancel by paying for the rest of the year. And it can only be done via desktop — wasted 30 minutes just trying to figure it out.

1 star. Never using this again and wouldn’t recommend it to anyone.

review:
I am filing this complaint because Whoop is providing inaccurate and misleading data through their product. Over the past few years, I have reached out to the company multiple times to report that the sleep tracker was recording me as asleep when I was actually awake and walking around, which is clear evidence that the data is inaccurate.

Despite raising this concern, the company showed no interest in addressing the issue. Instead of investigating or acknowledging a potential bug in their system, they dismissed my concerns and simply provided a link to request a refund. This response suggests they are indifferent to the accuracy of their product and unwilling to address potential flaws.

My concern is that if the sleep data is inaccurate, then all other related data generated by the tracker is likely compromised, rendering the product unreliable. It is alarming that the company does not seem to care about the integrity of their data or the impact of providing false information to consumers.
title: Inaccurate data-beware

review: If you’ve ever had a fitness tracker like a Fitbit or Apple Watch and been underwhelmed by the depth, accuracy, or practicality of the data it produces, look no further. The interface is easy to use, is beautifully designed, and has tons of useful features and insight. I legitimately cannot recommend WHOOP enough, and if there is anyone on the fence about getting one, I promise you will not be disappointed! The WHOOP coach feature is incredibly useful, and something I legitimately use daily. I’ve had a WHOOP for a little over a year now, and I can confidently say it has improved my life, made me more mindful about my health and lifestyle, and helped me to both set and achieve goals for myself. I could go on and on, but the through line is GET. A. WHOOP.

review:
Amazing when it first came out but seriously…
TLDR: in 3 years the only meaningful update was journal+correlations on software, a bunch of clothes, and huge endorsements. Otherwise, I’m unsure what whoop is doing. They only need a 20 person team to do what they have accomplished.

My feedback:
- App needs a huge overhaul. It is one of the messiest and redundant-laden pieces of software on my device. It’s just a huge collection of descriptive statistics with a touch of correlations. 
- monthly and weekly reports need overhaul. Anyone with any experience with charts will tell you that there’s much more insightful ways of presenting the data
- Sensors need improvement. The accuracy, especially when doing any activity, is grossly delayed and inaccurate vs Polar (electro) or even Apple Watch (optical, like Whoop)
- Straps are an insane cash grab. Priced similar to Apple Watch straps, but last 6-8 mo vs my Apple ones last 2-3 years
- ‘AI’ is a joke. Mucks up answers most of the time. I exported data => GPT => asked same questions and the insights were FAR more useful
- Where is the 5.0? I’ve been paying $250/yr for 4 years now and only got the 4.0 upgrade…. Vs. Apple went from near-useless to full blown Ultra with similar functionality as Whoop with 3rd party apps in the same timeframe
- blah blah blah
title: Going downhill
